summary: Gruber investigates why iOS 11's smart punctuation feature stopped working in the Messages app. He traces the likely cause to SMS's antiquated character encoding limits: ASCII-only messages allow 160 characters, but a single curly quote forces 16-bit encoding, cutting the limit to 70. He questions why this SMS limitation was also applied to iMessage (blue bubble) conversations, which have no such constraints. The story ends well: iOS 11.3 beta restores smart punctuation for iMessages while correctly disabling it only for SMS messages.
key_points: iOS 11 added smart punctuation (curly quotes, em-dashes) that macOS had for years, and Gruber notes the delay was hard to justify
SMS uses 7-bit GSM encoding allowing 160 characters, but any non-ASCII character forces 16-bit encoding, halving capacity to 70 characters
Smart punctuation was disabled in all Messages conversations, not just SMS, which Gruber identifies as an overcorrection
iOS 11.3 beta fixes the issue by applying smart punctuation to iMessages while disabling it only for green-bubble SMS
Gruber notes a remaining edge case: users who already write in languages requiring 16-bit encoding gain nothing from disabling smart punctuation in SMS
money_quotes: I say "finally" because MacOS has had the feature in the standard text editing system for many years, and I can't think of a good reason why it wasn't in iOS years ago.

I can say it's not a difficult programming job to solve because I've solved it in the more difficult context of smartening punctuation in prose without messing up the necessary dumb quotes inside HTML tags.
In short: if you stick to dumb quotes, you can put 160 characters in an SMS message. Include just one smart/curly quote, and you only get 70 characters.

But if I'm right about why, then why does it apply to iMessage messages — a.k.a. blue-bubble messages — too? iMessage messages aren't limited by the antiquated constraints of SMS in any other way, so why limit them typographically?
key_insight: Apple's fix for SMS character-encoding limits was an overcorrection that needlessly degraded typography in iMessage, and the proper solution was distinguishing between the two protocols.
